EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with a weak low pressure system,(1010 mb) which traveled through the Gulf of Maine on the afternoon of the 16th. Northeasterly marine winds subsided from gale force during the afternoon to less than 10 kts near the time of high tide resulting in minimal coastal flooding. The tide reached 12.01 feet in Portland Harbor. What was left of Surf St. in Saco, (York County) was briefly inundated resulting in 10,000.00 in damage. No damage was reported in Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomically high tide combined with an area of weak low pressure to produce coastal flooding in York County. Surf St. in Saco was briefly inundated resulting in 10,000.00 woth of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck several homes in York County.  Quick responses from homeowners, neighbors, and fire departments kept damages to a minimum.  Homes were struck in York Harbor, York, North Berwick and also in Cape Elizabeth in Cumberland County.  The most severe damage was to a large oceanfront home in York Harbor where lightning put two holes in the roof and ignited a fire which caused heavy damage to an upstairs bedroom.  Damage was estimated at $8000.

The remnants of hurricane Noel moved northeast through the eastern Gulf of Maine on the 3rd of November. Reports of high winds with this event were generally minimal. During the peak of the storm Central Maine Power only reported 5,172 customers without power. Most of these outages were in Rockland, Augusta, and Brunswick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 mph were reported by a spotter in Cape Elizabeth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center moved through central Maine Thursday November 15 dropping up to 3 inches of rain.  The rain caused some minor flooding in the Maine mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan where the flow reached 36050 cfs, slightly above the flood flow of 35000 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center moved through central Maine Thursday November 15 dropping up to 3 inches of rain.  The rain caused some minor flooding in the Maine mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported along Route 120 in Rumford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved through the Great Lakes on the morning of the 19th.  A secondary low developed off Cape Cod during the day on the 19th, the moved slowly northeast overnight and on the 20th.  A trough stretched westward from the low and served as a focus for heavy snow across southwestern Maine.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches across the area.

An intensifying coastal low lifting north through the Gulf of Maine to the maritime provinces brought heavy snow to portions of extreme eastern Maine. Heavy snow developed around the intensifying low generally producing 5 to 8 inches of snow accumulation across portions of extreme eastern Maine...with localized totals of around 10 inches across northern Washington county. The low continued to intensify while lifting through the maritimes...producing gusty winds which produced considerable blowing and drifting snow in the wake of the low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure across southern New England during the 14th...lifted north across the Gulf of Maine and eastern Maine during the night of the 14th and morning of the 15th. Snow developed and expanded north across the region during the 14th...becoming heavy at times. Across Downeast areas...enough warm air was drawn north to change the precipitation to sleet and freezing rain with a transition to all rain for a time along the immediate coast. Strong winds also affected the Downeast coast...where wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported. Some trees and power lines were toppled along the coast leading to power outages. Storm total snowfall across Downeast Maine generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with 1/2 to 1 inch of sleet and varying amounts of ice accumulation from freezing rain. Storm total snowfall across central and northern portions of the region generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with local totals up to around 18 inches. Considerable blowing and drifting snow also occurred across central and northern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Sleet accumulations of 1/2 to 1 inch were also reported along with varying amounts of ice accumulation from freezing rain. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensifying across southern New England during the afternoon of the 2nd...tracked northeast across Downeast Maine that night...then exited through the maritimes during the 3rd. Snow expanded north across the region during the morning of the 2nd. Snow...heavy at times...then continued across the region through much of the 2nd...with snow diminishing from south to north later on the 2nd through the early morning hours of the 3rd. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain across Downeast areas before ending. Gusty winds contributed to considerable blowing and drifting snow. The strongest winds occurred along the Downeast coast where gusts up to 55 to 65 mph were reported. A 71 mph wind gust was reported at an elevated recording site on Mount Desert Island in the Gulf of Maine. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with sleet accumulations of up to around an inch near the Downeast coast. Storm total snow accumulations across northern areas also generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals to around 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals of around 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while tracking north along the coast during the 16th...then continued lifting north across Maine during the 17th. Snow developed across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 16th then expanded north across the rest of the region through the rest of that afternoon and evening. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain across most of the region before ending from south to north during the 17th. Across Downeast areas...the precipitation transitioned to heavy rain before ending. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with several inches of sleet also occurring along with up to a half inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain near the Downeast coast. Storm total snow accumulations across central and northeast portions of the region generally ranged from 5 to 9 inches...with several inches of sleet also occurring. The heaviest snows fell across northwest portions of the region...to the west of the lows track...where snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches with local totals to around 14 inches along with some sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals of around 14 inches. Sleet accumulations of 1 to 1.5 inches were also reported along with some freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while tracking across Maine to the Maritime Provinces generally bringing 2 to 5 inches of snow...with locally higher totals...to northern Maine. Strong gusty winds developed across the region in the wake of the intensifying low. The combination of newly fallen snow and strong gusty winds produced localized whiteout conditions...with near zero visibilities at times. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common across the region through the afternoon and evening of the 20th. The combination of whiteout conditions and slippery roads contributed to a 15 car accident on Route 1 just south of Caribou during the early afternoon. The accident produced two non-life threatening injuries and closed the road for nearly an hour while vehicles were cleared. The localized whiteouts and slippery road conditions also contributed to other accidents across the region through the afternoon and evening.

Low pressure intensified while tracking north along the coast during the 16th...then continued lifting north across Maine during the 17th. Snow developed across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 16th then expanded north across the rest of the region through the rest of that afternoon and evening. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain across most of the region before ending from south to north during the 17th. Across Downeast areas...the precipitation transitioned to heavy rain before ending. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with several inches of sleet also occurring along with up to a half inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain near the Downeast coast. Storm total snow accumulations across central and northeast portions of the region generally ranged from 5 to 9 inches...with several inches of sleet also occurring. The heaviest snows fell across northwest portions of the region...to the west of the lows track...where snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches with local totals to around 14 inches along with some sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Sleet accumulations of 1 to 1.5 inches were also reported along with some freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore winds developed along the Downeast coast to the east of low pressure lifting north across Maine. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common along the coast...with some gusts to around 65 mph. A 66 mph wind gust was reported at Lubec with a 61 mph wind gust reported at West Quoddy. The strong winds brought down trees...branches and power lines which contributed to power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred at Pembroke in Washington county where around 1300 customers lost power for a time. Other scattered power outages were reported along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred. The strong winds contributed to scattered power outages.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure across southern New England during the 14th...lifted north across the Gulf of Maine and eastern Maine during the night of the 14th and morning of the 15th. Snow developed and expanded north across the region during the 14th...becoming heavy at times. Across Downeast areas...enough warm air was drawn north to change the precipitation to sleet and freezing rain with a transition to all rain for a time along the immediate coast. Strong winds also affected the Downeast coast...where wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported. Some trees and power lines were toppled along the coast leading to power outages. Storm total snowfall across Downeast Maine generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with 1/2 to 1 inch of sleet and varying amounts of ice accumulation from freezing rain. Storm total snowfall across central and northern portions of the region generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with local totals up to around 18 inches. Considerable blowing and drifting snow also occurred across central and northern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches. Sleet accumulations of 1/2 to 1 inch were also reported along with varying amounts of ice accumulation from freezing rain. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure across southern New England during the 14th...lifted north across the Gulf of Maine and eastern Maine during the night of the 14th and morning of the 15th. Snow developed and expanded north across the region during the 14th...becoming heavy at times. Across Downeast areas...enough warm air was drawn north to change the precipitation to sleet and freezing rain with a transition to all rain for a time along the immediate coast. Strong winds also affected the Downeast coast...where wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported. Some trees and power lines were toppled along the coast leading to power outages. Storm total snowfall across Downeast Maine generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with 1/2 to 1 inch of sleet and varying amounts of ice accumulation from freezing rain. Storm total snowfall across central and northern portions of the region generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with local totals up to around 18 inches. Considerable blowing and drifting snow also occurred across central and northern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with local totals of around 18 inches. |

Low pressure intensified while tracking across Maine to the Maritime Provinces generally bringing 2 to 5 inches of snow...with locally higher totals...to northern Maine. Strong gusty winds developed across the region in the wake of the intensifying low. The combination of newly fallen snow and strong gusty winds produced localized whiteout conditions...with near zero visibilities at times. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common across the region through the afternoon and evening of the 20th. The combination of whiteout conditions and slippery roads contributed to a 15 car accident on Route 1 just south of Caribou during the early afternoon. The accident produced two non-life threatening injuries and closed the road for nearly an hour while vehicles were cleared. The localized whiteouts and slippery road conditions also contributed to other accidents across the region through the afternoon and evening.

An area of low pressure located over the Upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning 3rd moved northeastward to the Great Lakes on the morning of the 4th.  A secondary low formed along the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 4th, then moved slowly northeastward along the coast and was located in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 5th and then in northern Maine by the morning of the 6th.  The late season storm brought heavy snow to the state.  Snowfall amounts along the coast ranged from 4 to 8 inches with inland and mountain areas getting 8 to 22 inches of snow.

Snow developed across Downeast Maine during the afternoon of the 4th in advance of low pressure tracking northeast along the coast toward the Gulf of Maine. The snow became heavy at times Downeast overnight from the 4th to the 5th...while slowly expanding north across the region. Snow began during the morning of the 5th across extreme northern portions of the region...becoming heavy at times. The low tracked from southeast Maine across western New Brunswick during the 5th. Snow...heavy at times...continued across the region through much of the afternoon of the 5th before ending from south to north. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with 10 to 16 inches across central areas and 9 to 13 inches across extreme northern areas. Heavy wet snow accumulating on trees snapped branches and weighed down other branches to the point where they contacted power lines. The heavy snow also brought down some power lines. The most extensive power outages occurred across central and Downeast areas where nearly 6000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time. Power outages were also reported across northern areas...though in much lower numbers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 17 inches.|

Storm total snow accumulations Downeast ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with 10 to 16 inches across central areas and 9 to 13 inches across extreme northern areas. Heavy wet snow accumulating on trees snapped branches and weighed down other branches to the point where they contacted power lines. The heavy snow also brought down some power lines. The most extensive power outages occurred across central and Downeast areas where nearly 6000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 14 to 18 inches.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Great Lakes in combination with a second low that developed off the mid-Atlantic coast brought heavy snow to the region.  Along the immediate coast, the snow changed to rain before ending.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches along the coast of York County to more than 6 inches in other parts of the region, with more than a foot in some mountain locations.
Strong onshore winds developed in advance of an occluded front crossing the region. Wind gusts to around 60 mph were reported across portions of coastal Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to around 60 mph occurred along the immediate coast...with a measured wind gust of 62 mph at Lubec.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon in the vicinity of a slow moving frontal boundary. Several of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail. The most significant storm developed a bow echo with damaging winds which tracked from central Piscataquis county to northcentral Penobscot county. Additional severe thunderstorms lingered across Downeast areas into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled near the Henderson Checkpoint by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

Intensifying low pressure tracking along the Maine coast to the maritimes from late on the 16th through the morning of the 17th brought heavy precipitation and strong winds to northern and eastern Maine. Across extreme northern areas precipitation remained mostly snow...occasionally mixing with sleet. Storm total snow accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from 9 to 14 inches...with localized totals to around 17 inches. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting of the snow. Across central portions of the region the snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain...with storm total snow accumulations ranging from 7 to 11 inches along with some ice accumulation. Across Downeast areas the snow transitioned to sleet and freezing rain...with all rain developing for a time along the coast. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches...with localized totals around 10 inches. Ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were common Downeast...with localized totals of up to around an inch.|Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred across interior Downeast areas. Strong onshore wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common along the Downeast coast...with some gusts to around 65 mph. The combination of  heavy wet snow and ice accumulating on trees and power lines along with the strong winds led to power outages. Several thousand customers lost power across Downeast areas for varying amounts of time. These were many of the same areas which had been effected during the storm several weeks earlier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 11 inches. Ice accumulations ranged from 1/8 to 1/4 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from the afternoon of the 3rd through the morning of the 4th brought heavy precipitation and gusty winds to northern and eastern Maine. The storm brought heavy snow to the entire region. Across northern and central areas storm total snowfall generally ranged from 10 to 16 inches...with lesser totals of 5 to 8 inches across northwest areas. Winds occasionally gusting in excess of 30 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting of the snow. Heavier precipitation occurred across Downeast areas...nearer the track of the low. Storm total snowfall across Downeast areas generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with localized totals across portions of Washington county approaching 24 inches.|Enough warm air was drawn inland to allow precipitation Downeast to mix with sleet and rain at times. The heavy wet snow weighed down branches which in some instances came into contact with power lines or snapped and fell on power lines contributing to power outages. Heavy wet snow accumulating on the power lines also contributed to power outages. The combination of the heavy wet snow and winds which occasionally gusted to around 40 mph snapped branches and toppled some trees and power lines which had been weighted down by snow. In excess of 1600 customers lost power for varying lenghts of time...with the most extensive power outages reported across Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 18 inches.

Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from the afternoon of the 3rd through the morning of the 4th brought heavy precipitation and gusty winds to northern and eastern Maine. The storm brought heavy snow to the entire region. Across northern and central areas storm total snowfall generally ranged from 10 to 16 inches...with lesser totals of 5 to 8 inches across northwest areas. Winds occasionally gusting in excess of 30 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting of the snow. Heavier precipitation occurred across Downeast areas...nearer the track of the low. Storm total snowfall across Downeast areas generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with localized totals across portions of Washington county approaching 24 inches.|Enough warm air was drawn inland to allow precipitation Downeast to mix with sleet and rain at times. The heavy wet snow weighed down branches which in some instances came into contact with power lines or snapped and fell on power lines contributing to power outages. Heavy wet snow accumulating on the power lines also contributed to power outages. The combination of the heavy wet snow and winds which occasionally gusted to around 40 mph snapped branches and toppled some trees and power lines which had been weighted down by snow. In excess of 1600 customers lost power for varying lenghts of time...with the most extensive power outages reported across Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in advance of a cold front crossing Maine. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common across Downeast areas. Higher gusts occurred with onshore winds near the coast where winds occasionally gusted to around 60 mph. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was reported at Cutler in coastal Washington county. The winds toppled some trees and power lines leading to power outages...with around a thousand customers losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Old Town leading to power outages.

Across central portions of the region the snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain...with storm total snow accumulations ranging from 7 to 11 inches along with some ice accumulation. Across Downeast areas the snow transitioned to sleet and freezing rain...with all rain developing for a time along the coast. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches...with localized totals around 10 inches. Ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were common Downeast...with localized totals of up to around an inch.|Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred across interior Downeast areas. Strong onshore wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common along the Downeast coast...with some gusts to around 65 mph. The combination of  heavy wet snow and ice accumulating on trees and power lines along with the strong winds led to power outages. Several thousand customers lost power across Downeast areas for varying amounts of time. These were many of the same areas which had been effected during the storm several weeks earlier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 11 to 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from the afternoon of the 3rd through the morning of the 4th brought heavy precipitation and gusty winds to northern and eastern Maine. The storm brought heavy snow to the entire region. Across northern and central areas storm total snowfall generally ranged from 10 to 16 inches...with lesser totals of 5 to 8 inches across northwest areas. Winds occasionally gusting in excess of 30 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting of the snow. Heavier precipitation occurred across Downeast areas...nearer the track of the low. Storm total snowfall across Downeast areas generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with localized totals across portions of Washington county approaching 24 inches.|Enough warm air was drawn inland to allow precipitation Downeast to mix with sleet and rain at times. The heavy wet snow weighed down branches which in some instances came into contact with power lines or snapped and fell on power lines contributing to power outages. Heavy wet snow accumulating on the power lines also contributed to power outages. The combination of the heavy wet snow and winds which occasionally gusted to around 40 mph snapped branches and toppled some trees and power lines which had been weighted down by snow. In excess of 1600 customers lost power for varying lenghts of time...with the most extensive power outages reported across Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in advance of a cold front crossing Maine. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common across Downeast areas. Higher gusts occurred with onshore winds near the coast where winds occasionally gusted to around 60 mph. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was reported at Cutler in coastal Washington county. The winds toppled some trees and power lines leading to power outages...with around a thousand customers losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and a utility pole were blown down in the Sedgwick area.

Low pressure lifting north across Maine brought 3 to 6 inches of snow to Downeast areas...before warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to heavy rain. Storm total liquid precipitation...from a combination of melted snow and rain...generally ranged from 1.50 to 2.50 inches across the region with local totals of around 3.00 inches. The warmer air also began melting the snowpack which provided additional runoff to rivers and streams. Some smaller streams overflowed their banks...particularly in low lying and poor drainage areas...which blocked some roads. In addition...snow blocking storm drains led to street flooding and areas of standing water. Several roads were damaged due to flooding. Route 1 was washed out to a depth of 3 feet in spots near Robbinston in Washington county. Roads around Bucksport and Tremont in Hancock county were also damaged. Around Bucksport problems were reported on Route 1...River Road...Millvale Road and Town Farm Road along with several steets in town. Around Tremont problems were reported on Cape Road...Kellytown Road and Turner Road. Basements were also flooded across the entire region.

Low pressure lifting north across Maine brought 3 to 6 inches of snow to Downeast areas...before warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to heavy rain. Storm total liquid precipitation...from a combination of melted snow and rain...generally ranged from 1.50 to 2.50 inches across the region with local totals of around 3.00 inches. The warmer air also began melting the snowpack which provided additional runoff to rivers and streams. Some smaller streams overflowed their banks...particularly in low lying and poor drainage areas...which blocked some roads. In addition...snow blocking storm drains led to street flooding and areas of standing water. Several roads were damaged due to flooding. Route 1 was washed out to a depth of 3 feet in spots near Robbinston in Washington county. Roads around Bucksport and Tremont in Hancock county were also damaged. Around Bucksport problems were reported on Route 1...River Road...Millvale Road and Town Farm Road along with several steets in town. Around Tremont problems were reported on Cape Road...Kellytown Road and Turner Road. Basements were also flooded across the entire region.

Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from late on the 12th through the 13th brought heavy snow to the region. Snow began during the late afternoon and evening of the 12th across Downeast areas then expanded north early that night...becoming heavy at times. Snow...heavy at times...then continued through the morning of the 13th before ending. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches across much of the region. Slightly lesser accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported across northwest areas...furthest from the track of the low. Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were also reported across southeast portions of the region...where the snow mixed with some sleet and rain. Heavy wet snow weighing down branches and power lines contributed to power outages. Several thousand customers across the region lost power for varying lengths of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved from the Ohio Valley the evening of the 14th, across southern New England on the 15th, and into the Canadian maritimes.  The storm brought 4 to 8 inches of snow to the mountains and foothills of Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving eastward from the Ohio Valley brought 6 to 8 inches of snow to the mountains of Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along the southeast U.S. coast on the morning of March 16. Strong cold high pressure was anchored over southern Canada resulting in cold temperatures over the northeast. As low pressure moved north up the coast, precipitation began as snow over Maine. By the morning of the 17th, a strong low pressure center was approaching southern New England. At this time, about 6 inches of heavy wet snow had fallen over Waldo, Lincoln, and Knox counties. However, warm air began moving inland off the water and quickly changed the snow to heavy rain as temperatures warmed from the 20s to the upper 40s in just 2 hours, melting the snow in a very short period of time. In addition, 2 to 3 inches of rain fell on top of the melting snow causing rapid runoff and much flooding. Many roads and culverts were washed out, as well as damage to some bridge crossings. A federal disaster (FEMA-1691-DR) was declared for the Maine counties of Lincoln, Knox and Waldo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt caused extensive flooding of small rivers and streams in Jefferson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the southeastern U.S. coast of the United States on the morning of the 16th, and then intensified as it moved slowly northeast to southern New England by the morning of the 17th, and then to the St. Lawrence River by the morning of the 18th.  The storm brought 7 to 10 inches of snow across much of the coast and interior, with more than a foot in the mountains.  The snow changed to a rain and/or freezing rain across coastal and interior locations, with heavy rain in the mid coast area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the southeast U.S. coast on the morning of March 16th. It intensified while heading north overnight, approaching southern New England early on the 17th before passing north-northeastward through the Gulf of Maine.  The pounding surf caused by the storm caused $100,000 in damage from from Surf Street to Main Avenue in Saco.  At Wells Beach, the surf caused beach erosion and plows were brought in to plow rocks off the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf caused $100,000.00 worth of damage from from Surf St. to Main Ave. in Saco.

An area of low pressure developed off the southeastern U.S. coast of the United States on the morning of the 16th, and then intensified as it moved slowly northeast to southern New England by the morning of the 17th, and then to the St. Lawrence River by the morning of the 18th.  The storm brought 7 to 10 inches of snow across much of the coast and interior, with more than a foot in the mountains.  The snow changed to a rain and/or freezing rain across coastal and interior locations, with heavy rain in the mid coast area.

Low pressure formed along the southeast U.S. coast on the morning of March 16. Strong cold high pressure was anchored over southern Canada resulting in cold temperatures over the northeast. As low pressure moved north up the coast, precipitation began as snow over Maine. By the morning of the 17th, a strong low pressure center was approaching southern New England. At this time, about 6 inches of heavy wet snow had fallen over Waldo, Lincoln, and Knox counties. However, warm air began moving inland off the water and quickly changed the snow to heavy rain as temperatures warmed from the 20s to the upper 40s in just 2 hours, melting the snow in a very short period of time. In addition, 2 to 3 inches of rain fell on top of the melting snow causing rapid runoff and much flooding. Many roads and culverts were washed out, as well as damage to some bridge crossings. A federal disaster (FEMA-1691-DR) was declared for the Maine counties of Lincoln, Knox and Waldo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt caused extensive flooding of small rivers and streams in Prospect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore winds developed along the Downeast coast to the east of low pressure lifting north across Maine. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common along the coast...with some gusts to around 65 mph. A 66 mph wind gust was reported at Lubec with a 61 mph wind gust reported at West Quoddy. The strong winds brought down trees...branches and power lines which contributed to power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred at Pembroke in Washington county where around 1300 customers lost power for a time. Other scattered power outages were reported along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common...with some gusts to around 65 mph. A 66 mph wind gust was reported at Lubec with a 61 mph wind gust reported at West Quoddy. The strong winds contributed to power outages. Around 1300 customers lost power for a time at Pembroke...with scattered power outages elsewhere.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting north across Maine brought 3 to 6 inches of snow to Downeast areas...before warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to heavy rain. Storm total liquid precipitation...from a combination of melted snow and rain...generally ranged from 1.50 to 2.50 inches across the region with local totals of around 3.00 inches. The warmer air also began melting the snowpack which provided additional runoff to rivers and streams. Some smaller streams overflowed their banks...particularly in low lying and poor drainage areas...which blocked some roads. In addition...snow blocking storm drains led to street flooding and areas of standing water. Several roads were damaged due to flooding. Route 1 was washed out to a depth of 3 feet in spots near Robbinston in Washington county. Roads around Bucksport and Tremont in Hancock county were also damaged. Around Bucksport problems were reported on Route 1...River Road...Millvale Road and Town Farm Road along with several steets in town. Around Tremont problems were reported on Cape Road...Kellytown Road and Turner Road. Basements were also flooded across the entire region.

Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from late on the 12th through the 13th brought heavy snow to the region. Snow began during the late afternoon and evening of the 12th across Downeast areas then expanded north early that night...becoming heavy at times. Snow...heavy at times...then continued through the morning of the 13th before ending. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches across much of the region. Slightly lesser accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported across northwest areas...furthest from the track of the low. Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were also reported across southeast portions of the region...where the snow mixed with some sleet and rain. Heavy wet snow weighing down branches and power lines contributed to power outages. Several thousand customers across the region lost power for varying lengths of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.|

An area of low pressure located over the Upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning 11th moved northeastward to the Great Lakes on the morning of the 12th.  A secondary low formed along the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 12th, then moved slowly northeastward along the coast and was located in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 13th and then southeast of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th. An area of low pressure over the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th, rapidly intensified as it slowly moved north to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th. This intense low in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced Overview: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved slowly from the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th.  The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop.  The combination of astronomical high tides, storm surge, and large waves contributed to coastal flooding in coastal areas of the state and severe coastal erosion in areas south of Portland.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region which, when combined with snow melt, produced widespread flooding across much of the region.  The flooding of small rivers and streams was worst in southern and coastal areas and led to numerous road closures.   In the mountains, the rain was preceded by heavy snow.  Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided.  However, rain continued into Tuesday across the region and each high tide during the period brought a reoccurrence of coastal flooding and erosion to the area.  Power outages persisted, and stream and river flooding continued across the region during this period.  Many roads remained closed.|On Friday, April 20, high pressure built eastward from the Great Lakes ending the influence of the Atlantic storm.  |  |Wind: The pressure gradient associated with the storm brought high winds to the region.  The damage observed in the hardest hit areas was typical of wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph.  While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides.  In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday. In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified while tracking from the southeastern states to the southern New England coast from the 15th to the 16th. A tight pressure gradient developed between the low and high pressure centered over eastern Canada which also blocked the northern movement of the low. The intense low slowly drifted east from the 16th through the 19th while high pressure remained across eastern Canada. The storm had many impacts on the region including strong damaging winds...heavy rain with urban and small stream flooding and coastal tidal flooding. These impacts were most significant across Downeast areas...particularly near the coast. In addition...the storm brought locally heavy snow to some higher elevations of northern and northwest Maine.|The tight pressure gradient supported strong east to northeast winds across mostly central and Downeast portions of the region through the 16th and 17th. Disturbances rotating around the main low contributed to several surges of strong winds over the course of the event. Winds gradually diminished during the 18th and 19th...though gusty winds still lingered during this time particularly near the Downeast coast.|Damaging winds occurred across all of Downeast Maine...particularly near the coast where some wind gusts exceeded 70 mph. Damaging winds extended inland to central Piscataquis...central Penobscot and northern Washington counties. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from the heavy rains which accompanied the storm and the spring thaw contributed to the uprooting of numerous trees with many other trees snapped and broken. Several trees fell on structures and vehicles causing damage. Other trees and branches brought down powerlines when they fell...with power poles also snapped and toppled due to the strong winds which contributed to thousands of power outages. Fallen trees and power lines also blocked roads.|The combination of astronomical high tides...amongst the highest of the year...along with persistent strong onshore winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding during high tide cycles starting on the evening of the 16th and continuing through the evening of the 19th. During high tides...overwash flooded some coastal roads resulting in road closures. Debris was washed onto roads with some roads also damaged. A causeway connecting the mainland with Deer Isle was closed several times during the event due to overwash and flooding.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region. The heaviest rains occurred across central and Downeast areas...along with the favored upslope areas of Piscataquis county. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. Across Piscataquis county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heavy rain contributed to rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams across the region some of which overflowed onto surrounding low land areas. The small river and stream flooding caused some road closures. Urban and poor drainage flooding also occurred which led to additional road closures and contributed to basement flooding. Mainstem rivers also rose during this time but crested below flood stage.|Enough cold air was in place to support an interval of heavy snow during the early stages of the event across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county and northern Somerset county. Snowfall totals across some higher terrain areas ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals in excess of 12 inches. Lesser snow acumulations occurred at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds uprooted or broke numerous trees...along with snapping many branches across the region. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common...with some gusts to around 60 mph. Several intervals of particularly strong winds occurred. The first episode of stronger winds occurred during the afternoon of the 16th which produced the initial wind damage. The second episode of stronger winds...generally the strongest of the event...occurred during the night of the 16th producing further damage. The third episode of particularly strong winds occurred during the afternoon of the 17th. The uprooted trees and snapped branches falling on power lines...with many utility poles also toppled due to the winds...contributed to extensive power outages. Many roads were blocked due to fallen trees...utility poles and power lines. A tree fell on a vehicle in Dedham during the morning of the 17th injuring the driver.

During high tides...overwash flooded some coastal roads resulting in road closures. Debris was washed onto roads with some roads also damaged. The heaviest rains occurred across central and Downeast areas...along with the favored upslope areas of Piscataquis county. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. Across Piscataquis county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heavy rain contributed to rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams across the region some of which overflowed onto surrounding low land areas. The small river and stream flooding caused some road closures. Urban and poor drainage flooding also occurred which led to additional road closures and contributed to basement flooding. Mainstem rivers also rose during this time but crested below flood stage.|Enough cold air was in place to support an interval of heavy snow during the early stages of the event across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county and northern Somerset county. Snowfall totals across some higher terrain areas ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals in excess of 12 inches. Lesser snow acumulations occurred at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds uprooted or broke numerous trees...along with snapping many branches across the region. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common...with some gusts to around 60 mph. Several intervals of particularly strong winds occurred. The first episode of stronger winds occurred during the afternoon of the 16th which produced the initial wind damage. The second episode of stronger winds...generally the strongest of the event...occurred during the night of the 16th producing further damage. The third episode of particularly strong winds occurred during the afternoon of the 17th. The uprooted trees and snapped branches falling on power lines...with many utility poles also toppled due to the winds...contributed to extensive power outages. Many roads were blocked due to fallen trees...utility poles and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th, rapidly intensified as it slowly moved north to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th. This intense low in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced Overview: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved slowly from the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th.  The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop.  The combination of astronomical high tides, storm surge, and large waves contributed to coastal flooding in coastal areas of the state and severe coastal erosion in areas south of Portland.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region which, when combined with snow melt, produced widespread flooding across much of the region.  The flooding of small rivers and streams was worst in southern and coastal areas and led to numerous road closures.   In the mountains, the rain was preceded by heavy snow.  Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided.  However, rain continued into Tuesday across the region and each high tide during the period brought a reoccurrence of coastal flooding and erosion to the area.  Power outages persisted, and stream and river flooding continued across the region during this period.  Many roads remained closed.|On Friday, April 20, high pressure built eastward from the Great Lakes ending the influence of the Atlantic storm.  |  |Wind: The pressure gradient associated with the storm brought high winds to the region.  The damage observed in the hardest hit areas was typical of wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph.  While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides.  In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday.  Rainfall in Southern Maine was near the 100 year recurrence interval at Sanford and Eliot.|Flooding:  The heavy rain combined with snow melt to cause flooding of small rivers and streams in much of southern and central Maine.   The worst flooding occurred on the smaller rivers in York County.  Damage to infrastructure was severe (roads, bridges, waste water treatment plants, public buildings). Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Androscoggin River at Auburn, (flood stage 13.0 ft.), crested at 13 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt contributed to elevated river levels across the region which dislodged ice leading to ice jams. Snow melt occurred throughout the weekend of the 21st and 22nd which led to rising water levels on area rivers and streams. Record warmth on Monday the 23rd...with temperatures in the 70s...led to very rapid snow melt and sharp rises on rivers and streams which dislodged ice. Ice jams began to develop later on the 23rd and continued across the region through the afternoon of the 25th while ice was flushed through area rivers and streams. The most significant ice jams occurred along the Saint John River. An ice jam on the Saint John River formed near the Allagash and Saint Francis town line during the late afternoon of the 24th flooding a half mile stretch of Route 161 and covering the road with ice resulting in closure of that portion of the road. The ice jam released later that night allowing river levels to recede. Even though portions of the road were damaged...the road was able to be re-opened once the ice was cleared the next morning. Another ice jam briefly formed on the Saint John River at Fort Kent causing the river to rise to a stage of around 20 feet...where flood stage is 25 feet. The ice jam released after several hours moving further down river. The ice continued moving along the Saint John River from Madawaska...to Grand Isle...to Van Buren overnight on the 24th through the afternoon of the 25th. The elevated river levels and several ice jams led to some flooding of lowland areas along this portion of the river through the afternoon of the 25th. Although river levels remained elevated...much of the ice had either flushed through the river or been grounded on river banks by late afternoon of the 25th. Lesser ice jams and elevated water levels on the Aroostook river and other tributaries of both the Aroostook and Saint John Rivers resulted in lowland flooding across portions of the region during this time.

The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop. Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided. While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides. EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th, rapidly intensified as it slowly moved north to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th. This intense low in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced Overview: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved slowly from the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th.  The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop.  The combination of astronomical high tides, storm surge, and large waves contributed to coastal flooding in coastal areas of the state and severe coastal erosion in areas south of Portland.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region which, when combined with snow melt, produced widespread flooding across much of the region.  The flooding of small rivers and streams was worst in southern and coastal areas and led to numerous road closures.   In the mountains, the rain was preceded by heavy snow.  Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided.  However, rain continued into Tuesday across the region and each high tide during the period brought a reoccurrence of coastal flooding and erosion to the area.  Power outages persisted, and stream and river flooding continued across the region during this period.  Many roads remained closed.|On Friday, April 20, high pressure built eastward from the Great Lakes ending the influence of the Atlantic storm.  |  |Wind: The pressure gradient associated with the storm brought high winds to the region.  The damage observed in the hardest hit areas was typical of wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph.  While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides.  In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday.  Rainfall in Southern Maine was near the 100 year recurrence interval at Sanford and Eliot.|Flooding:  The heavy rain combined with snow melt to cause flooding of small rivers and streams in much of southern and central Maine.   The worst flooding occurred on the smaller rivers in York County.  Damage to infrastructure was severe (roads, bridges, waste water treatment plants, public buildings). Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified while tracking from the southeastern states to the southern New England coast from the 15th to the 16th. A tight pressure gradient developed between the low and high pressure centered over eastern Canada which also blocked the northern movement of the low. The intense low slowly drifted east from the 16th through the 19th while high pressure remained across eastern Canada. The storm had many impacts on the region including strong damaging winds...heavy rain with urban and small stream flooding and coastal tidal flooding. These impacts were most significant across Downeast areas...particularly near the coast. In addition...the storm brought locally heavy snow to some higher elevations of northern and northwest Maine.|The tight pressure gradient supported strong east to northeast winds across mostly central and Downeast portions of the region through the 16th and 17th. Disturbances rotating around the main low contributed to several surges of strong winds over the course of the event. Winds gradually diminished during the 18th and 19th...though gusty winds still lingered during this time particularly near the Downeast coast.|Damaging winds occurred across all of Downeast Maine...particularly near the coast where some wind gusts exceeded 70 mph. Damaging winds extended inland to central Piscataquis...central Penobscot and northern Washington counties. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from the heavy rains which accompanied the storm and the spring thaw contributed to the uprooting of numerous trees with many other trees snapped and broken. Several trees fell on structures and vehicles causing damage. Other trees and branches brought down powerlines when they fell...with power poles also snapped and toppled due to the strong winds which contributed to thousands of power outages. Fallen trees and power lines also blocked roads.|The combination of astronomical high tides...amongst the highest of the year...along with persistent strong onshore winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding during high tide cycles starting on the evening of the 16th and continuing through the evening of the 19th. During high tides...overwash flooded some coastal roads resulting in road closures. Debris was washed onto roads with some roads also damaged. A causeway connecting the mainland with Deer Isle was closed several times during the event due to overwash and flooding.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region. The heaviest rains occurred across central and Downeast areas...along with the favored upslope areas of Piscataquis county. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. Across Piscataquis county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heavy rain contributed to rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams across the region some of which overflowed onto surrounding low land areas. The small river and stream flooding caused some road closures. Urban and poor drainage flooding also occurred which led to additional road closures and contributed to basement flooding. Mainstem rivers also rose during this time but crested below flood stage.|Enough cold air was in place to support an interval of heavy snow during the early stages of the event across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county and northern Somerset county. Snowfall totals across some higher terrain areas ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals in excess of 12 inches. Lesser snow acumulations occurred at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds uprooted or broke numerous trees...along with snapping many branches across the region. Wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common...with some gusts to around 70 mph near the immediate coast.|Some measured gusts included 72 mph at Cutler...53 mph at Eastport|and 48 mph at Milbridge. Several intervals of particularly strong winds occurred. The first episode of stronger winds occurred during the afternoon of the 16th which produced the initial wind damage. The second episode of stronger winds...generally the strongest of the event...occurred during the night of the 16th producing further damage. The third episode of particularly strong winds occurred during the afternoon of the 17th. The numerous uprooted trees and snapped branches falling on power lines...with many utility poles also toppled due to the winds...contributed to extensive power outages. Many roads were blocked due to fallen trees...utility poles and power lines.  Structural damage occurred to homes due to falling trees. Many structures also suffered various degrees of damage to roofs and siding...with many signs also damaged. In Eastport windows were blown out of several buildings along the waterfront during the evening of the 16th. A roof was torn off a mobile home in Lubec. Several harbors suffered damage to piers and docks due to the strong winds and rough seas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure located over the Upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning 11th moved northeastward to the Great Lakes on the morning of the 12th.  A secondary low formed along the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 12th, then moved slowly northeastward along the coast and was located in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 13th and then southeast of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th.  The late season storm brought heavy snow to much of the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches along the coast to 10 to 15 inches in the mountains.

A combination of rain and melting snow contributed to rising water levels on the Mattawamkeag River at Mattawamkeag. The river reached flood stage...13.0 feet...during the morning of the 23rd. Record warmth occurred across the region during the 23rd causing rapid snow melt and runoff contributing to further increases in river levels. The river then remained above flood stage into the early morning hours of the 30th. During this time river levels were generally between 13 and 14 feet...with the river cresting at just over 14 feet during the evening of the 26th. Only minor flooding occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and melting snow contributed to rising water levels on the Mattawamkeag River at Mattawamkeag. Only minor flooding occurred while the river was above flood stage...mainly affecting low lying areas along the river.

In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday.  Rainfall in Southern Maine was near the 100 year recurrence interval at Sanford and Eliot.|Flooding:  The heavy rain combined with snow melt to cause flooding of small rivers and streams in much of southern and central Maine.   The worst flooding occurred on the smaller rivers in York County.  Damage to infrastructure was severe (roads, bridges, waste water treatment plants, public buildings). Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified while tracking from the southeastern states to the southern New England coast from the 15th to the 16th. A tight pressure gradient developed between the low and high pressure centered over eastern Canada which also blocked the northern movement of the low. The intense low slowly drifted east from the 16th through the 19th while high pressure remained across eastern Canada. The storm had many impacts on the region including strong damaging winds...heavy rain with urban and small stream flooding and coastal tidal flooding. These impacts were most significant across Downeast areas...particularly near the coast. In addition...the storm brought locally heavy snow to some higher elevations of northern and northwest Maine.|The tight pressure gradient supported strong east to northeast winds across mostly central and Downeast portions of the region through the 16th and 17th. Disturbances rotating around the main low contributed to several surges of strong winds over the course of the event. Winds gradually diminished during the 18th and 19th...though gusty winds still lingered during this time particularly near the Downeast coast.|Damaging winds occurred across all of Downeast Maine...particularly near the coast where some wind gusts exceeded 70 mph. Damaging winds extended inland to central Piscataquis...central Penobscot and northern Washington counties. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from the heavy rains which accompanied the storm and the spring thaw contributed to the uprooting of numerous trees with many other trees snapped and broken. Several trees fell on structures and vehicles causing damage. Other trees and branches brought down powerlines when they fell...with power poles also snapped and toppled due to the strong winds which contributed to thousands of power outages. Fallen trees and power lines also blocked roads.|The combination of astronomical high tides...amongst the highest of the year...along with persistent strong onshore winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding during high tide cycles starting on the evening of the 16th and continuing through the evening of the 19th. During high tides...overwash flooded some coastal roads resulting in road closures. Debris was washed onto roads with some roads also damaged. A causeway connecting the mainland with Deer Isle was closed several times during the event due to overwash and flooding.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region. The heaviest rains occurred across central and Downeast areas...along with the favored upslope areas of Piscataquis county. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. Across Piscataquis county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heavy rain contributed to rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams across the region some of which overflowed onto surrounding low land areas. The small river and stream flooding caused some road closures. Urban and poor drainage flooding also occurred which led to additional road closures and contributed to basement flooding. Mainstem rivers also rose during this time but crested below flood stage.|Enough cold air was in place to support an interval of heavy snow during the early stages of the event across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county and northern Somerset county. Snowfall totals across some higher terrain areas ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals in excess of 12 inches. Lesser snow acumulations occurred at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm brought heavy rain to the region...particularly across southern portions of the county and favored upslope areas. Storm total rainfall across the county generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heaviest rains fell across southern portions of the county where the flooding was reported. The heavy rains caused rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams...some of which overflowed. Flooding of smaller rivers and streams...along with poor drainage and lowland flooding occurred across southern portions of the county. Flooding led to the closure of Route 7 between Dover Foxcroft and Dexter during the evening of the 16th. Some basements were also flooded. The larger mainstem rivers rose...but crested below flood stage.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure located over the Upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning 11th moved northeastward to the Great Lakes on the morning of the 12th.  A secondary low formed along the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 12th, then moved slowly northeastward along the coast and was located in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 13th and then southeast of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th.  The late season storm brought heavy snow to much of the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches along the coast to 10 to 15 inches in the mountains.
