EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure located over the Upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning 11th moved northeastward to the Great Lakes on the morning of the 12th.  A secondary low formed along the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 12th, then moved slowly northeastward along the coast and was located in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 13th and then southeast of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th.  The late season storm brought heavy snow to much of the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches along the coast to 10 to 15 inches in the mountains.

An area of low pressure rapidly intensified while tracking from the southeastern states to the southern New England coast from the 15th to the 16th. A tight pressure gradient developed between the low and high pressure centered over eastern Canada which also blocked the northern movement of the low. The intense low slowly drifted east from the 16th through the 19th while high pressure remained across eastern Canada. The storm had many impacts on the region including strong damaging winds...heavy rain with urban and small stream flooding and coastal tidal flooding. These impacts were most significant across Downeast areas...particularly near the coast. In addition...the storm brought locally heavy snow to some higher elevations of northern and northwest Maine.|The tight pressure gradient supported strong east to northeast winds across mostly central and Downeast portions of the region through the 16th and 17th. Disturbances rotating around the main low contributed to several surges of strong winds over the course of the event. Winds gradually diminished during the 18th and 19th...though gusty winds still lingered during this time particularly near the Downeast coast.|Damaging winds occurred across all of Downeast Maine...particularly near the coast where some wind gusts exceeded 70 mph. Damaging winds extended inland to central Piscataquis...central Penobscot and northern Washington counties. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from the heavy rains which accompanied the storm and the spring thaw contributed to the uprooting of numerous trees with many other trees snapped and broken. Several trees fell on structures and vehicles causing damage. Other trees and branches brought down powerlines when they fell...with power poles also snapped and toppled due to the strong winds which contributed to thousands of power outages. Fallen trees and power lines also blocked roads.|The combination of astronomical high tides...amongst the highest of the year...along with persistent strong onshore winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding during high tide cycles starting on the evening of the 16th and continuing through the evening of the 19th. During high tides...overwash flooded some coastal roads resulting in road closures. Debris was washed onto roads with some roads also damaged. A causeway connecting the mainland with Deer Isle was closed several times during the event due to overwash and flooding.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region. The heaviest rains occurred across central and Downeast areas...along with the favored upslope areas of Piscataquis county. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. Across Piscataquis county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heavy rain contributed to rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams across the region some of which overflowed onto surrounding low land areas. The small river and stream flooding caused some road closures. Urban and poor drainage flooding also occurred which led to additional road closures and contributed to basement flooding. Mainstem rivers also rose during this time but crested below flood stage.|Enough cold air was in place to support an interval of heavy snow during the early stages of the event across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county and northern Somerset county. Snowfall totals across some higher terrain areas ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals in excess of 12 inches. Lesser snow acumulations occurred at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations of up to 8 to 12 inches occurred across higher terrain areas.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th, rapidly intensified as it slowly moved north to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th. This intense low in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced Overview: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved slowly from the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th.  The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop.  The combination of astronomical high tides, storm surge, and large waves contributed to coastal flooding in coastal areas of the state and severe coastal erosion in areas south of Portland.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region which, when combined with snow melt, produced widespread flooding across much of the region.  The flooding of small rivers and streams was worst in southern and coastal areas and led to numerous road closures.   In the mountains, the rain was preceded by heavy snow.  Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided.  However, rain continued into Tuesday across the region and each high tide during the period brought a reoccurrence of coastal flooding and erosion to the area.  Power outages persisted, and stream and river flooding continued across the region during this period.  Many roads remained closed.|On Friday, April 20, high pressure built eastward from the Great Lakes ending the influence of the Atlantic storm.  |  |Wind: The pressure gradient associated with the storm brought high winds to the region.  The damage observed in the hardest hit areas was typical of wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph.  While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides.  In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday.  Rainfall in Southern Maine was near the 100 year recurrence interval at Sanford and Eliot.|Flooding:  The heavy rain combined with snow melt to cause flooding of small rivers and streams in much of southern and central Maine.   The worst flooding occurred on the smaller rivers in York County.  Damage to infrastructure was severe (roads, bridges, waste water treatment plants, public buildings). Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half the roof taken off of the Sea Pier Lobster Pound in Booth Bay. Roof ripped off barn on Browns Farm in Nobelboro. Numerous trees and power lines down.

The heavy rains caused rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams...some of which overflowed. Flooding of smaller rivers and streams...along with poor drainage and lowland flooding occurred across the entire county. Flooding led to the closure of many roads across the county. Road closures and flooding were reported at Route 166 in Castine...Route 230 in Trenton...portions of Route 1A in Ellsworth...Route 204 in Lamoine at Archers Brook and Fly Point Road in Brooklin. Road closures were also reported on Mount Desert Island including Route 3 at Seal Harbor...Bettys Road and Gilbert Farm Road near Bar Harbor along with Route 102 and Seawall Road at Southwest Harbor. Coastal flooding also contributed to the closure of some roads on Mount Desert Island. The flooding damaged some roads and bridges. Some basements were also flooded. The larger mainstem rivers rose...but crested below flood stage.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified while tracking from the southeastern states to the southern New England coast from the 15th to the 16th. A tight pressure gradient developed between the low and high pressure centered over eastern Canada which also blocked the northern movement of the low. The intense low slowly drifted east from the 16th through the 19th while high pressure remained across eastern Canada. The storm had many impacts on the region including strong damaging winds...heavy rain with urban and small stream flooding and coastal tidal flooding. These impacts were most significant across Downeast areas...particularly near the coast. In addition...the storm brought locally heavy snow to some higher elevations of northern and northwest Maine.|The tight pressure gradient supported strong east to northeast winds across mostly central and Downeast portions of the region through the 16th and 17th. Disturbances rotating around the main low contributed to several surges of strong winds over the course of the event. Winds gradually diminished during the 18th and 19th...though gusty winds still lingered during this time particularly near the Downeast coast.|Damaging winds occurred across all of Downeast Maine...particularly near the coast where some wind gusts exceeded 70 mph. Damaging winds extended inland to central Piscataquis...central Penobscot and northern Washington counties. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from the heavy rains which accompanied the storm and the spring thaw contributed to the uprooting of numerous trees with many other trees snapped and broken. Several trees fell on structures and vehicles causing damage. Other trees and branches brought down powerlines when they fell...with power poles also snapped and toppled due to the strong winds which contributed to thousands of power outages. Fallen trees and power lines also blocked roads.|The combination of astronomical high tides...amongst the highest of the year...along with persistent strong onshore winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding during high tide cycles starting on the evening of the 16th and continuing through the evening of the 19th. During high tides...overwash flooded some coastal roads resulting in road closures. Debris was washed onto roads with some roads also damaged. A causeway connecting the mainland with Deer Isle was closed several times during the event due to overwash and flooding.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region. The heaviest rains occurred across central and Downeast areas...along with the favored upslope areas of Piscataquis county. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. Across Piscataquis county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches with local totals approaching 5.00 inches. The heavy rain contributed to rapid rises on smaller rivers and streams across the region some of which overflowed onto surrounding low land areas. The small river and stream flooding caused some road closures. Urban and poor drainage flooding also occurred which led to additional road closures and contributed to basement flooding. Mainstem rivers also rose during this time but crested below flood stage.|Enough cold air was in place to support an interval of heavy snow during the early stages of the event across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county and northern Somerset county. Snowfall totals across some higher terrain areas ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals in excess of 12 inches. Lesser snow acumulations occurred at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds uprooted or broke trees...along with snapping many branches across the region. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common...with some gusts to around 60 mph. The uprooted trees and snapped branches falling on power lines...with some utility poles also toppled due to the winds...contributed to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th, rapidly intensified as it slowly moved north to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th. This intense low in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced Overview: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved slowly from the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th.  The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop.  The combination of astronomical high tides, storm surge, and large waves contributed to coastal flooding in coastal areas of the state and severe coastal erosion in areas south of Portland.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region which, when combined with snow melt, produced widespread flooding across much of the region.  The flooding of small rivers and streams was worst in southern and coastal areas and led to numerous road closures.   In the mountains, the rain was preceded by heavy snow.  Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided.  However, rain continued into Tuesday across the region and each high tide during the period brought a reoccurrence of coastal flooding and erosion to the area.  Power outages persisted, and stream and river flooding continued across the region during this period.  Many roads remained closed.|On Friday, April 20, high pressure built eastward from the Great Lakes ending the influence of the Atlantic storm.  |  |Wind: The pressure gradient associated with the storm brought high winds to the region.  The damage observed in the hardest hit areas was typical of wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph.  While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides.  In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday.  Rainfall in Southern Maine was near the 100 year recurrence interval at Sanford and Eliot.|Flooding:  The heavy rain combined with snow melt to cause flooding of small rivers and streams in much of southern and central Maine.   The worst flooding occurred on the smaller rivers in York County.  Damage to infrastructure was severe (roads, bridges, waste water treatment plants, public buildings). Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large battering waves to produce severe coastal flooding across most of the Maine coast. It was the seventh highest tide ever recorded in Portland Harbor in modern day history (13.28 feet). The Fort Point tide gage reached 12.15 feet. Many roads were closed and residents evacuated. Damage to marinas, buoys, docks and coastal property was significant. Channel markers went adrift from their 20,000 pound blocks, causing navigation problems for mariners. The Portland Large Navigational buoy was heavily damaged while measuring 32 foot waves and was later found drifting near the Isles of Shoals. Water and waves were over the roads along the coast, including Saco and York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure located over the Upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning 11th moved northeastward to the Great Lakes on the morning of the 12th.  A secondary low formed along the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 12th, then moved slowly northeastward along the coast and was located in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 13th and then southeast of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th.  The late season storm brought heavy snow to much of the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches along the coast to 10 to 15 inches in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho crossed northern Maine during the morning and early afternoon hours. Strong winds associated with the derecho caused structural damage and extensive tree damage. Thousands of trees were toppled or snapped across Aroostook County and northern portions of Penobscot...Piscataquis and Somerset counties. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common with the derecho...with localized wind gusts of 75 to 80 mph. This was the most extensive convective wind event to affect the region in many years. In the wake of the derecho...additional thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon in advance of an approaching cold front. This second round of thunderstorms affected mostly central and Downeast portions of the region producing damaging winds and large hail. These thunderstorms persisted through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled across the town...leading to numerous power outages...by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. Some structural and roof damage was also reported.

Thousands of trees were toppled or snapped across Aroostook County and northern portions of Penobscot...Piscataquis and Somerset counties. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common with the derecho...with localized wind gusts of 75 to 80 mph. This was the most extensive convective wind event to affect the region in many years. In the wake of the derecho...additional thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon in advance of an approaching cold front. This second round of thunderstorms affected mostly central and Downeast portions of the region producing damaging winds and large hail. These thunderstorms persisted through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Route 1 between Grand Isle and Madawaska by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees were toppled or snapped at Madawaska Lake by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph. A pontoon boat located at a dock was flipped by the winds. While most of the damage was attributed to straight line winds...a brief gustnado possibly occurred on the leading edge of the squall line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late morning and afternoon in association with an upper level disturbance and cold pool aloft crossing the region. With the cold air aloft...the main impact of the thunderstorms was large hail. One particularly severe storm crossing the Saint John Valley produced 2.00 to 2.50 inch hail in the Fort Kent area...damaging many vehicles in Fort Kent. Hail in excess of 1.00 inch was common across northern Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail significantly damaged many vehicles across town. At a car dealership nearly every vehicle on the lot suffered significant damage with broken windows and large dents. Many other vehicles across the town also received varying degrees of damage due to broken windows and dents from the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed across northern Maine in advance of an approaching cold front. One storm on the northern end of the squall line developed supercell characteristics. This supercell eventually produced a tornado...microburst and large hail. Other isolated severe thunderstorms also developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size increased from 0.75 inch to 1.50 inch. The storm produced a tornado several minutes after the report of 1.50 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed across northern Maine in advance of an approaching cold front. One storm on the northern end of the squall line developed supercell characteristics. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred across the southern end of Long Lake in conjunction with a tornado which also tracked across the region. Both the tornado and microburst had winds estimated at around 65 knots (75 mph). The combination of tornadic winds and straight-line winds associated with the microburst toppled or snapped hundreds of trees. Several cabins and cottages sustained minor damage to roofs and siding due to a combination of the winds and falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed across the Saint John Valley producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground.

A squall line developed across northern Maine in advance of an approaching cold front. One storm on the northern end of the squall line developed supercell characteristics. This supercell eventually produced a tornado...microburst and large hail. Other isolated severe thunderstorms also developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed near the west shoreline of Long Lake...crossed the southern end of the lake...then moved back over land across the east shoreline of the lake before dissipating. The total path length of the tornado was around 1.0 mile. Hundreds of trees were uprooted or snapped along the track of the tornado with many trees also de-barked (DI 27, DOD 3). Several cabins and cottages received minor damage to roofs and siding due to the combination of winds and falling trees. Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated around 65 knots (75 mph). The average path width was 125 yards. A microburst also occurred in conjunction with the tornado producing straight-line winds also estimated around 65 knots (75 mph). The damage to trees and structures was due to a combination of tornadic and straight-line winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported at Munsungan Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho crossed northern Maine during the morning and early afternoon hours. Strong winds associated with the derecho caused structural damage and extensive tree damage. Thousands of trees were toppled or snapped across Aroostook County and northern portions of Penobscot...Piscataquis and Somerset counties. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common with the derecho...with localized wind gusts of 75 to 80 mph. This was the most extensive convective wind event to affect the region in many years. In the wake of the derecho...additional thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon in advance of an approaching cold front. This second round of thunderstorms affected mostly central and Downeast portions of the region producing damaging winds and large hail. These thunderstorms persisted through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and evening in the vicinity of a nearly stationary frontal boundary. Several of the thunderstorms became severe...producing damaging winds and large hail. A camper at Baxter State Park was killed during the evening after being struck by lightning...with his brother injured by the same lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One camper was killed and his brother injured when lightning struck a tree near their campsite and arced to strike them both where they were sheltering. The incident occurred at the Katahdin Stream Campground in Baxter State Park at some point between 8:00 and 8:15 PM. The surviving brother was hospitalized with first and second degree burns to his feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed across northern Maine in advance of an approaching cold front. One storm on the northern end of the squall line developed supercell characteristics. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled across Presque Isle by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine in advance of a slow moving cold front. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 0.75 inch was reported at Madawaska Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine in advance of a slow moving cold front. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled at Madawaska Lake by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th, rapidly intensified as it slowly moved north to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th. This intense low in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced Overview: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved slowly from the southeastern United States on the morning of Sunday, April 15th to near New York City by the morning of Monday, April 16th.  The intense low over New York City in combination with high pressure over eastern Canada produced a very intense pressure gradient across the area which caused strong east to northeast winds to develop across the region.  Over land, the strong winds downed numerous trees.  The downed trees caused widespread power outages, especially near the coast, and numerous road closures.  Over the marine areas, the strong winds caused a storm surge and large ocean waves of up to 30 feet to develop.  The combination of astronomical high tides, storm surge, and large waves contributed to coastal flooding in coastal areas of the state and severe coastal erosion in areas south of Portland.|The storm also brought heavy rain to the region which, when combined with snow melt, produced widespread flooding across much of the region.  The flooding of small rivers and streams was worst in southern and coastal areas and led to numerous road closures.   In the mountains, the rain was preceded by heavy snow.  Flooding was minor on most mainstem rivers, however, in southern Maine, the Presumpscot River reached near record levels. |The intense low drifted very slowly eastward from Monday morning through Friday morning as it gradually weakened.  During this time, the pressure gradient slowly relaxed, the winds gradually diminished, and the seas slowly subsided.  However, rain continued into Tuesday across the region and each high tide during the period brought a reoccurrence of coastal flooding and erosion to the area.  Power outages persisted, and stream and river flooding continued across the region during this period.  Many roads remained closed.|On Friday, April 20, high pressure built eastward from the Great Lakes ending the influence of the Atlantic storm.  |  |Wind: The pressure gradient associated with the storm brought high winds to the region.  The damage observed in the hardest hit areas was typical of wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph.  While most observing sites reported winds below this range, topography, elevation, and exposure were likely factors in determining the amount of wind damage any location experienced.  In addition, the heavy rain brought strong wind gusts to the ground level and contributed to the sporadic nature of the heaviest wind damage.  In addition to the meteorological factors, damage from the wind was exacerbated because of the heavy rain that accompanied the wind and the soft, wet ground caused by the spring thaw.|The wind knocked over numerous trees and branches throughout the region and caused numerous and prolonged power outages throughout the region.| |Coastal Flooding/Storm Surge/Waves:  The coastal storm hit during a period of very high (and very low) astronomical tides.  In addition to the astronomical tides, the prolonged period of strong northeast winds caused a prolonged storm surge along the coastline.  Also during this period, the strong winds caused large and battering waves along the coast.  The storm tides and large waves caused significant flooding and coastal erosion. |In Portland, flood level is 12 feet.  During the storm, storm tides reached or exceeded the 12 foot flood level during 7 tidal cycles.  In addition, due to the large waves, coastal erosion occurred throughout the week of April 15th to 20th when the Portland gauge was above or near the 12 foot flood level.  Communication was lost with NOAA���s Portland Weather Buoy shortly after 6 am Monday when it was reporting 31 foot seas.|Precipitation:  Rain developed across the southern part of the state early Sunday afternoon, April 15th and spread northward.  The rain became heavy during the afternoon and overnight.  By Monday morning, 3 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in York County with rainfall amounts decreasing to 1 to 4 inches to the north and east.    In the mountains of Maine, 2 to 7 inches of snow fell by Monday morning.  Although the heaviest precipitation fell from Sunday afternoon into Monday afternoon, precipitation persisted into Tuesday.  Rainfall in Southern Maine was near the 100 year recurrence interval at Sanford and Eliot.|Flooding:  The heavy rain combined with snow melt to cause flooding of small rivers and streams in much of southern and central Maine.   The worst flooding occurred on the smaller rivers in York County.  Damage to infrastructure was severe (roads, bridges, waste water treatment plants, public buildings). Homes and businesses were also damaged. In York County, two people were killed when they attempted to cross a flooded road on foot and were swepted away. The crest on the Presumpscot River was just below the crest recorded during Hurricane Bob in August of 1991.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides in combination with strong northeast winds and large ocean waves continued to cause coastal flooding in portions of Maine as the remnants of the the Patriots Day storm slowly drifted east, well southeast of the Gulf of Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front crossing northern and eastern Maine. Several thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were broken by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Downeast Maine along a frontal boundary. Additional evening thunderstorms developed along the primary cold front crossing central and Downeast areas. Large hail and damaging winds occurred with some thunderstorms through the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled on Foxcroft Center Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Downeast Maine along a frontal boundary. Large hail and damaging winds occurred with some thunderstorms through the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled on Williamsburg Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Downeast Maine along a frontal boundary. Additional evening thunderstorms developed along the primary cold front crossing central and Downeast areas. Large hail and damaging winds occurred with some thunderstorms through the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of varying size lasted for nearly 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rolled across western Maine on the afternoon of July 6, producing numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the chimney of a house in Lewiston and blew it apart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rolled across western Maine on the afternoon of July 6, producing numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm blew large tree limbs down onto wires in Rockland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rolled across western Maine on the afternoon of July 6, producing numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm blew down trees on Rocky Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in southeast New Hampshire and quickly moved into southwestern Maine on the morning of the 27th producing large hail and damaging winds. Lightning struck an abandoned house being used for storage in Sabattus during the early afternoon and burned it to the ground.  An isolated severe thunderstorm also affected Franklin County in northern Maine in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a smoky fire in an ocean-front home in South Bristol.  The fire struck some nearby trees and apparently entered the home through the electrical system.  The fire smoldered for several hours before it was discovered.  Damage is estimated at $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds ahead of a cold front caused isolated damage across portions of western Maine.  A strong wind gust blew down a swath of trees in the town of North Berwick.  Some houses in the immediate area sustained minor damage.  Another strong wind gust tore the roof off a mobile home in Sidney.  No other damage was reported in either area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly wind gust ahead of an approaching cold front tore the roof off a mobile home in Sidney.  No other damage was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds ahead of a cold front caused isolated damage across portions of western Maine.  A strong wind gust blew down a swath of trees in the town of North Berwick.  Some houses in the immediate area sustained minor damage.  Another strong wind gust tore the roof off a mobile home in Sidney.  No other damage was reported in either area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly wind gust ahead of an approaching cold front blew down a swath of trees in the town of North Berwick. Some houses in the area sustained minor damage.  No other damage was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from early morning thunderstorms caused damage in Jefferson.  Lightning struck and ignited a fire that destroyed two outbuildings at a home in the town.  Damage was estimated at $100,000.

Thunderstorms started producing severe weather as early as 9:50 am EDT, and by the end of the day there were numerous severe thunderstorms with reports of large hail and damaging winds across much of western Maine. The severe thunderstorm activity lasted into late evening. There were many reports of private property damage due to strong winds downing trees and numerous reports of large hail, some up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and power lines in East Madison. Dozens of pine trees were snapped off. Both pines and hardwoods were uprooted. Multiple homes suffered roof damage. Several docks were overturned and boats tossed around on Wesserunsett Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms began developing in northwestern Maine early in the afternoon of August 30th and continued into the evening hours over central portions of western Maine. Very large hail and damaging winds occurred with these storms along with . There were many reports of 1 to 2 inch hail.  A supercell thunderstorm which developed over southeastern Oxford County moved through northern Kennebec County. Golf ball-size hail caused considerable damage and piled up to a depth of 10 inches in the town of Rome in Kennebec county. Snow plows had to be called out to clear roads, and several inches of hail remained on the ground as late as the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and branches at Wilson Lake Country Club. Half inch hail was also reported with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracking across Hancock county during the evening hours produced a non-continuous swath of wind damage over a roughly two mile wide path from Castine to near Blue Hill...a distance of 10 to 15 miles. The most extensive damage was reported near Castine where a microburst produced estimated wind gusts of 85 to 95 mph. Other pockets of enhanced wind damage occurred along the path of the storm with wind gusts estimated at 70 to 75 mph. The winds caused extensive tree damage and some structural damage along the storm path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst in the Castine area produced estimated wind gusts of 85 to 95 mph. Numerous large trees across the town were uprooted or snapped by the winds...with significant damage also reported to power lines and utility poles. Several structures and five vehicles were damaged when trees were toppled on to them. Many roads across the town were blocked for a time immediately after the storm due to the large number of fallen trees and power lines. On the campus of the Maine Maritime Academy several buildings sustained roof damage and broken windows...with damage also reported to bleachers at the football field. It was estimated that storm clean up across town could take several weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a series of thunderstorms fell in steep terrain and caused flash flooding. Rainfall estimates from Doppler RADAR indicated that 6 to 8 inches of rain fell in 3 hours. The most damage occurred in Bethel where a reservoir on Chapman Brook was destroyed. This reservoir was the public water supply for Bethel.  Route 26 in Newry, as well as the Upper Skiway Road at the Sunday River Ski Area, were washed out. A woman was rescued from her car after it was carried off the Summit Road when Barker Brook flooded at the Sunday River Ski Area. Route 113 in Gilead was closed due to road washouts and landslides. A bridge on Route 113 was damaged. Route 2 in Bethel was closed due to a washed out culvert. Wild River Road was washed out in two places stranding several campers at Wild River Campground in the White Mountain National Forest. Both the Wild River Campground and Hastings Campground were inaccessible during and after the flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across southern Maine to the maritimes brought a wintry mix of precipitation to northern Maine during Thanksgiving. The precipitation started in the form of snow during the early morning hours...then eventually mixed with sleet...freezing rain and rain before ending. Snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches occurred across northern portions of Aroostook county...with the heavier accumulations generally across northern and higher terrain areas. Sleet and freezing rain accumulations ranged from one-quarter to three-quarters of an inch. The combination of snow and ice broke some tree limbs which fell on power lines contributing to scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches...with the greater totals generally occurring across northern and higher terrain areas. Sleet and freezing rain accumulations ranged from one-quarter to three-quarters of an inch.||||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in southeast New Hampshire and quickly moved into southwestern Maine on the morning of the 27th producing large hail and damaging winds. Lightning struck an abandoned house being used for storage in Sabattus during the early afternoon and burned it to the ground.  An isolated severe thunderstorm also affected Franklin County in northern Maine in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Alfred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting across eastern Maine drew cold air south across northwest Maine allowing rain to change to heavy snow before ending. The heaviest snows generally fell near the Quebec border and across the higher elevations of the region. Snow totals of 4 to 8 inches were common across the region...with localized totals of up to 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common. Localized totals of 9 to 12 inches occurred along the Quebec border.||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense extratropical low Noel brought strong winds and heavy rains to portions of the region while tracking north across the Gulf of Maine and maritime provinces. The strongest winds occurred across central and Downeast portions of the region from the evening of Saturday the 3rd through the early morning hours of Sunday the 4th. The strong winds  toppled and snapped many trees along with large branches. The combination of strong winds and falling trees also brought down many power lines and utility poles which contributed to thousands of people losing power for varying lengths of time. Sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph...with gusts of 60 to 70 mph...occurred across central and Downeast areas. The strongest winds generally occurred along the coast...nearest the track of the low.||The storm also brought heavy rain to portions of the region. Storm total rainfall of 2.00 to 3.00 inches was common across central and Downeast areas...with localized totals of 4.00 to 5.00 inches along portions of the Downeast coast. The heavy rains contributed to some poor drainage flooding...which was compounded by leaves blocking storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph.||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense extratropical low Noel brought strong winds and heavy rains to portions of the region while tracking north across the Gulf of Maine and maritime provinces. The strongest winds occurred across central and Downeast portions of the region from the evening of Saturday the 3rd through the early morning hours of Sunday the 4th. The strong winds  toppled and snapped many trees along with large branches. The combination of strong winds and falling trees also brought down many power lines and utility poles which contributed to thousands of people losing power for varying lengths of time. Sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph...with gusts of 60 to 70 mph...occurred across central and Downeast areas. The strongest winds generally occurred along the coast...nearest the track of the low.||The storm also brought heavy rain to portions of the region. Storm total rainfall of 2.00 to 3.00 inches was common across central and Downeast areas...with localized totals of 4.00 to 5.00 inches along portions of the Downeast coast. The heavy rains contributed to some poor drainage flooding...which was compounded by leaves blocking storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at around 65 mph. A wind gust to 64 mph was measured at Brooklin.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting across eastern Maine drew cold air south across northwest Maine allowing rain to change to heavy snow before ending. The heaviest snows generally fell near the Quebec border and across the higher elevations of the region. Snow totals of 4 to 8 inches were common across the region...with localized totals of up to 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches occurred...mostly west of a line from Allagash to Clayton Lake. Localized totals of 9 to 12 inches occurred along the Quebec border. Lesser snow totals occurred across eastern portions of the region.||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late morning and afternoon in association with an upper level disturbance and cold pool aloft crossing the region. With the cold air aloft...the main impact of the thunderstorms was large hail. One particularly severe storm crossing the Saint John Valley produced 2.00 to 2.50 inch hail in the Fort Kent area...damaging many vehicles in Fort Kent. Hail in excess of 1.00 inch was common across northern Maine.

Thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon in the vicinity of a slow moving frontal boundary. Several of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail. The most significant storm developed a bow echo with damaging winds which tracked from central Piscataquis county to northcentral Penobscot county. Additional severe thunderstorms lingered across Downeast areas into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled near the Hedgehog Checkpoint by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.
